title: Executive Order 12615—Performance of Commercial Activities

flags.justification: Responsibility is distributed across agency heads, with OMB providing coordination and guidance rather than concentrated control.
Extremely detailed deadlines, a required senior-level coordinating official (Sec. 1(g)), and quarterly reporting to the President through OMB (Sec. 1(h)).
Multiple related sub-requirements (identification, study, budget estimates, job placement) all coherently serve one purpose: commercial-activity privatization review.
References the specific, established methodology of OMB Circular No. A-76, as revised, rather than creating a vague standalone standard.

Sec. 1(e)'s incentive-compensation provision requires OMB negotiation and is explicitly bounded to rewarding productivity gains, a deliberate and bounded incentive-alignment feature rather than a perverse one.
A standing federal procurement policy framework, evergreen by nature.

Sec. 5 states 'nothing in this Order shall be construed to confer a private right of action on any person' — an explicit foreclosure of judicial enforcement of the order's own requirements, the same pattern found in EO 12114.

Sec. 2(b) requires public publication of the commercial-activity inventory and study schedules, and Sec. 1(f) requires a job-placement program for affected employees — genuine external and protective mechanisms.

This is the historically significant 'A-76' competitive-sourcing framework, which became a decades-long, contentious feature of federal procurement policy cited in countless subsequent disputes and reviews.
OMB, OPM, and individual agency roles are clearly delineated and coordinated.
The no-private-right-of-action provision is captured under Flag 7; no separate coverage exemption is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 2/18 = 11.1%. The historically significant 'A-76' federal competitive-sourcing framework. Second confirmed instance of the no-private-right-of-action pattern in this corpus (see EO 12114).
